questions.question: Explain what a reference variable is in C++ and how it differs from a pointer. Provide an example where a reference is used to modify the original variable.
keyPoints: A reference is an alias (another name) for an existing variable, declared with the & operator.
References must be initialized when declared and cannot be reseated to refer to a different object.
Unlike pointers, references have no null value, no arithmetic, and are automatically dereferenced.
Example: int x = 10; int &ref = x; ref = 20; // changes x to 20.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a pointer to constant (const int *p) and a constant pointer (int * const p). Give code examples for each.
keyPoints: const int *p: the data pointed to cannot be changed through p, but p can point to different addresses.
int * const p: the pointer itself is constant; it must be initialized to an address and cannot be reassigned, though the data can be modified.
Syntax: const int *p = &a; int * const p = &b;
Example: const int *p = &x; // *p = 5 illegal; int * const q = &y; *q = 5; // ok.

questions.question: Write a C++ program that dynamically allocates an integer array of size n (entered by the user), fills it with random numbers, computes the sum and average, and then deallocates the memory using delete. Also explain the use of pointer arithmetic to access array elements.
keyPoints: Use 'int *arr = new int[n];' to allocate an array of n integers.
Loop through the array using pointer arithmetic (e.g., *(arr + i) = random;) or arr[i] to assign values.
Compute sum and average by iterating, e.g., sum += *(arr + i); after loop avg = (double)sum / n;
Deallocate with 'delete[] arr;' and explain that pointer arithmetic increments the address by sizeof(int) per element.

questions.question: Discuss the various ways to pass arguments to a function in C++: pass-by-value, pass-by-pointer, and pass-by-reference. Show how each works, including use of const references, and discuss the implications for performance and modification of arguments. Provide example function prototypes and illustrate with a call.
keyPoints: Pass-by-value: function receives a copy; original unchanged, potentially costly for large structures.
Pass-by-pointer: function receives address; can modify data, requires checking for null, uses pointer syntax.
Pass-by-reference: function receives an alias; can modify without explicit dereferencing, syntax clearer; const reference avoids modification.
Performance: pass-by-value copies data, pass-by-pointer and pass-by-reference pass address, thus faster for large objects; const reference offers safety.
